text: Hemoglobin (Hb) is a protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen throughout the body. Low hemoglobin may indicate anemia, while high levels can be due to dehydration or other conditions.
Normal hemoglobin levels are typically 13.8 to 17.2 g/dL for men and 12.1 to 15.1 g/dL for women.
HbA1c (glycated hemoglobin) reflects average blood sugar levels over the past 2 to 3 months. It is used to diagnose and monitor diabetes.
Normal HbA1c levels are below 5.7 percent. Levels between 5.7 and 6.4 percent indicate prediabetes, and 6.5 percent or higher indicate diabetes.
White blood cells (WBC) are part of the immune system and help fight infections. High WBC count may indicate infection or inflammation, while low levels may indicate immune suppression.
Platelets help in blood clotting. Low platelet count can lead to bleeding disorders, while high levels may increase risk of clotting.
Red blood cells (RBC) carry oxygen from lungs to tissues. Abnormal levels may indicate anemia or other blood disorders.
Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) measures the average size of red blood cells. It helps classify types of anemia.
Blood glucose levels indicate the amount of sugar in the blood. High levels may suggest diabetes, while low levels may cause hypoglycemia.
Cholesterol is a fatty substance in the blood. High levels of LDL cholesterol increase the risk of heart disease, while HDL cholesterol is considered protective.
